hero.headline:
See How Crowded Travel
Destinations Really Get
hero.description: PackedPlaces.com visualizes tourist crowdedness for 700+ destinations worldwide, week by week, powered by school holiday data and major events from 100+ countries.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.step1Text: We collect school holiday schedules from 127 regions using three data providers: hand-curated dates, the OpenHolidaysAPI, and the date-holidays library.
howItWorks.step2Text: Over 6,000 travel patterns connect source regions to destinations, weighted by real tourism statistics from UNWTO, Eurostat, and national tourism authorities.
howItWorks.step3Text: Over 50 major events — from the FIFA World Cup and Olympics to Oktoberfest and Carnival — are layered on top, boosting destination scores during event weeks.
howItWorks.step4Text: Raw traffic scores are divided by each destination's capacity — accounting for category and month-by-month peak season data. Demand is dampened by weather patterns like monsoons, extreme heat, and cyclone seasons. The result: how packed a place feels.

features.title: Features
features.crowdednessText: Measures congestion relative to capacity. A packed ski village scores higher than a busy metropolis.
features.hemisphereText: Each destination has month-by-month peak season data reflecting real-world conditions — monsoons, dry/wet seasons, extreme heat, typhoons, and polar winters. Demand drops sharply during off-season months.
features.categoriesText: Ski, beach, city, island, safari, desert, tropical, lake, nature, cultural, and mountain — each with tuned capacity.
features.boostText: 1.5x multiplier during school breaks captures the surge in travel demand when families hit the road.
features.timelineText: Scrub or animate through every week of the year to see crowdedness shift in real time.
features.providersText: Three cascading providers — manual, OpenHolidaysAPI, and date-holidays — ensure global school holiday coverage.
features.eventsText: Olympics, FIFA World Cup, Oktoberfest, Carnival, Grand Slams, and more — major events that drive millions of visitors are tracked and shown on the map.

data.title: Data & Methodology
data.populationText: Eurostat, US Census, ABS, UN World Population Prospects
data.tourismText: UNWTO Compendium, Eurostat overnight stays, NTTO, JNTO
data.eventsDataText: 50+ recurring and one-time events including Olympics, FIFA World Cup, festivals, and Grand Slams
data.geographyText: Coordinates, per-month peak season profiles, category assignment
data.capacityDescription: Each category has a peak and off-peak capacity. A monthly attractiveness model interpolates between them based on each destination's peak months, accounting for monsoons, dry/wet seasons, and other weather patterns.

openSource.title: Open Source
openSource.description: PackedPlaces.com is fully open source. Browse the code, report issues, or contribute new regions and destinations.

disclaimer.title: Data Disclaimer
disclaimer.body: The crowdedness scores and travel data shown on this map are calculated estimates based on publicly available information, including school holiday schedules, event calendars, and tourism statistics. While we strive for accuracy, the data may not perfectly reflect real-world conditions. PackedPlaces cannot be held responsible for any inaccuracies. Please use this information as a helpful guide, not as a guarantee.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.controllerText: PackedPlaces.com is operated by 10ideen.at. For any privacy-related inquiries, please use our contact form.
privacy.collectText: PackedPlaces.com is designed to collect as little personal data as possible. We do not require user accounts or logins. The only personal data we process comes from voluntary contact form submissions and anonymized analytics.
privacy.cookiesText: We use a single functional cookie to remember your language preference:
privacy.cookieLocale: Stores your preferred language (en, de, es, fr). Expires after 1 year.
privacy.analyticsText: We use Google Analytics 4 to understand how visitors use our site. Google Analytics collects anonymized usage data including pages visited, time on site, and general geographic region. No personally identifiable information is collected. You can opt out by using a browser extension or enabling Do Not Track.
privacy.contactDataText: When you submit our contact form, we collect your name, email address, and message. This data is sent via email using Resend and is used solely to respond to your inquiry. We do not store contact form submissions in a database or share them with third parties.
privacy.rightsText: Under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), you have the following rights regarding your personal data:
privacy.rightAccess: Right of access — request a copy of your personal data
privacy.rightRectification: Right to rectification — request correction of inaccurate data
privacy.rightErasure: Right to erasure — request deletion of your personal data
privacy.rightRestriction: Right to restriction — request limited processing of your data
privacy.rightPortability: Right to data portability — receive your data in a structured format
privacy.rightObjection: Right to object — object to processing of your personal data
privacy.retentionText: Contact form emails are retained only as long as needed to respond to your inquiry. Analytics data is retained according to Google Analytics default settings (14 months).
privacy.changesText: We may update this privacy policy from time to time. Changes will be posted on this page with an updated date.
privacy.contactText: If you have questions about this privacy policy or wish to exercise your GDPR rights, please reach out via our contact form.

contact.title: Contact Us
contact.subtitle: Have a question, suggestion, or want to report an issue? We'd love to hear from you.
contact.successText: Thank you for reaching out. We'll get back to you as soon as possible.
contact.errorEmail: Please enter a valid email address.
contact.errorServer: Something went wrong. Please try again later.
contact.errorTurnstile: Captcha verification failed. Please try again.

shell.mapDesc: Explore 700+ destinations on the interactive map

crowdedness.title: Crowdedness, Not Traffic
crowdedness.subtitle: We don't just count visitors — we measure how packed a place feels by dividing traffic by capacity. A tiny ski village with 50,000 tourists is far more crowded than a metropolis absorbing millions.
crowdedness.misleadingTitle: Why raw visitor counts are misleading
crowdedness.misleadingText: New York City attracts 60 million visitors per year but has massive infrastructure to absorb them. Hallstatt, a tiny Austrian lake village, gets just 1 million visitors — yet it's overwhelmed. Our crowdedness score captures this difference.
crowdedness.insightText: Hallstatt receives 60x fewer visitors than NYC, yet it feels 2.5x more crowded. By normalizing against infrastructure capacity, our score reflects the real experience on the ground.
crowdedness.scaleText: Scores range from 0% (deserted) to 100% (at breaking point). The color scale on the map makes it easy to spot the most packed places at a glance.
crowdedness.howCapacityText: Each of the 11 destination categories has a pre-assigned capacity value reflecting its real-world infrastructure — hotels, restaurants, transit, and physical space. Cities absorb the most traffic. Deserts and mountains absorb the least. Capacity also shifts between peak and off-peak seasons, because infrastructure (seasonal hotels, ski lifts, beach services) scales with demand.

seasonality.title: Weather-Aware Seasonality
seasonality.subtitle: Not every destination peaks in summer. Each of our 700+ destinations has month-by-month attractiveness data reflecting real weather patterns — monsoons, dry seasons, extreme heat, typhoons, and polar winters.
seasonality.demandText: Bali empties during monsoon season (November–March). Innsbruck fills up when snow falls (December–March). Barcelona's beaches peak in summer. Our model assigns each destination a set of "peak months" and computes an attractiveness factor that drives the crowdedness score up or down throughout the year.
seasonality.modelText: Each destination's peak months are defined based on real climate data. The model then applies three tiers of demand:
seasonality.peakMonthsDesc: Full demand during the best weather window
seasonality.shoulderMonthsDesc: Reduced demand in months adjacent to peak
seasonality.insightText: This 3-tier model means a beach destination like Barcelona sees a 20x demand swing between July (100%) and January (5%). This directly affects the crowdedness score — the same destination can go from "Packed" to "Quiet" depending on the month.
seasonality.monsoonDesc: Bali, Thailand, India — heavy rains suppress tourism Nov–Mar or Jun–Sep
seasonality.extremeHeatDesc: Dubai, Rajasthan, Atacama — summer temperatures make outdoor activities impossible
seasonality.snowSeasonsDesc: Alps, Hokkaido, Banff — ski resorts peak when snowfall is guaranteed
seasonality.cycloneDesc: Caribbean, Philippines, Fiji — storm seasons drive visitors away
seasonality.polarWintersDesc: Scandinavia, Iceland, Patagonia — extreme cold and short days limit access

categories.title: 11 Destination Categories
categories.subtitle: Every destination is classified into one of 11 categories — from bustling cities to remote deserts. Each category has carefully tuned capacity values that reflect real-world infrastructure differences.
categories.capacityChartText: The capacity value determines how much traffic a destination can absorb before feeling crowded. Cities handle 10x more pressure than desert destinations. During off-peak months, capacity drops — reflecting the closure of seasonal hotels, restaurants, and transport services.
categories.insightText: A ski resort's capacity drops from 1.5 to 0.3 in summer — a 5x reduction. This is why even modest summer hiking traffic can produce a noticeable crowdedness score for Alpine destinations.
categories.assignmentText: Each of the 700+ destinations in our database is manually classified based on its primary tourist appeal. Some destinations could fit multiple categories (e.g. Barcelona is both a city and a beach destination), but we assign the single most representative category to keep the model clean. Category assignment directly affects the capacity divisor, which in turn drives the crowdedness score.
categories.skiDesc: Extreme seasonality — lifts close in summer

holidayBoost.title: Holiday Boost Effect
holidayBoost.subtitle: When school's out, families travel. Our model applies a 1.5x multiplier to travel demand during school holidays, capturing the predictable surge that transforms quiet destinations into packed hotspots.
holidayBoost.howText: For each of the 127 source regions, we track school holiday schedules week by week. When a region is on school break, all travel patterns originating from that region receive a 1.5x multiplier. This means a destination popular with German families will see its score spike during Bavaria's summer holidays, while a destination popular with Australian families spikes during their December–January break.
holidayBoost.insightText: The 1.5x multiplier is applied per-region, not globally. So when German schools are on break but French schools aren't, only the German travel contribution gets boosted. This creates realistic, staggered demand waves across Europe rather than a single monolithic spike.
holidayBoost.staggeringText: School holidays don't happen everywhere at once. Even within a single country, regions stagger their breaks. For example, German states have different summer holiday dates spread across 6 weeks. This means the holiday boost effect creates a rolling wave of demand rather than a sudden spike — just like in reality.

timeline.title: Week-by-Week Timeline
timeline.subtitle: Travel demand isn't static. Our timeline lets you scrub through all 52 weeks of the year — or hit play to watch crowdedness patterns animate across the globe in real time.
timeline.snapshotsText: Every week, we recalculate the crowdedness score for all 700+ destinations. The inputs change week by week: which regions are on school holiday, which events are active, and how the monthly seasonality model modulates demand. The result is a continuous, evolving picture of global tourism pressure.
timeline.baliText: Peaks June–September when European summer holidays overlap with Bali's best weather. Monsoon season (Dec–Feb) brings a dramatic drop.
timeline.innsbruckText: A dominant winter ski season (Dec–Mar) and a quiet period before a brief late-summer hiking bump. The seasonal swing is extreme.
timeline.barcelonaText: A smooth bell curve peaking in late July when beach season, summer holidays, and warm weather all align. Winter is notably quiet.
timeline.insightText: By animating through the weeks, you can watch demand "flow" around the globe — from Northern Hemisphere ski resorts in winter, to European beaches in summer, to Southern Hemisphere destinations during their peak seasons. It reveals the global choreography of tourism.
timeline.controlsText: On the map, you can interact with the timeline in two ways:
timeline.scrubText: Drag the week slider to jump to any week of the year. The heatmap updates instantly, showing you exactly how crowded each destination is during that specific week.
timeline.animateText: Press play to auto-advance through all 52 weeks. Watch the heatmap colors shift across the map as seasons change and holiday waves sweep the globe.

holidayProviders.title: Multi-Provider Holidays
holidayProviders.subtitle: School holiday data is fragmented and hard to find. We use three cascading data providers to ensure coverage across 127 regions worldwide — from hand-curated dates for major markets to automated fallbacks for the rest.
holidayProviders.tiersText: When computing the holiday boost for a region, we check providers in priority order. If a higher-tier provider has data for that region, we use it. Otherwise, we fall through to the next tier. This ensures every region has holiday data while prioritizing accuracy for the most impactful tourism markets.
holidayProviders.manualDesc: Hand-researched school holiday dates for the most important tourism source regions. Covers major European countries, the US, Australia, Japan, and other high-volume travel markets.
holidayProviders.apiDesc: A structured API providing school holiday data for European countries and select global regions. Fills gaps where manual curation isn't available.
holidayProviders.libraryDesc: An open-source npm library covering 100+ countries. Used as a fallback for regions not covered by the first two tiers. May use public holidays as a proxy where school dates aren't available.
holidayProviders.insightText: The cascade approach means we can ship accurate data for Germany (where school holidays vary by state and are precisely dated) while still covering Brazil or India with reasonable approximations. As better data becomes available, we simply add it to a higher tier.
holidayProviders.whyTitle: Why school holidays matter so much
holidayProviders.whyText: Families with school-age children are constrained to travel during school breaks. This creates massive, predictable demand spikes. In Europe, where school holidays are staggered across regions, the effect is a rolling wave of demand rather than a single spike — making week-by-week tracking essential.
holidayProviders.statWeeks: Weeks of summer holidays per region

events.title: 50+ Major Events
events.subtitle: From the Olympics and FIFA World Cup to Oktoberfest and Carnival — we track major events that attract millions of visitors and add their impact to the crowdedness score.
events.addOnTitle: Events add on top of baseline demand
events.addOnText: Each event has a traffic boost value (0.1–1.0) that gets added to the destination's score during the event weeks. This stacks on top of the normal holiday and seasonality effects — so an event during peak season creates an even bigger spike.
events.insightText: The 2026 FIFA World Cup across North America will boost crowdedness scores for multiple cities simultaneously — NYC, LA, Mexico City, and Toronto will all see score spikes during June and July, on top of their normal summer holiday demand.
events.recurringText: Most events in our database are recurring — Oktoberfest happens every September, Wimbledon every June. But we also track one-time mega-events like the Olympics and FIFA World Cup, which move to different host cities. When you switch years on the map, you'll see these events shift to their new locations.
events.recurringDesc: Same destination every year: Oktoberfest (Munich), Carnival (Rio), Australian Open (Melbourne), Edinburgh Fringe, Tomorrowland.
events.oneTimeDesc: Moving host cities: Winter Olympics 2026 (Italy), FIFA World Cup 2026 (North America), Summer Olympics 2028 (Los Angeles).
